title: Chronic Pancreatitis
oneLiner: Progressive, irreversible inflammatory damage to the pancreas causing exocrine/endocrine failure; affects 5–12/100,000; pain, malabsorption, diabetes drive morbidity.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
- **Classic vignette:** A 55-year-old man with a 20-year history of heavy alcohol use presents with severe epigastric pain radiating straight through to his back. He leans forward over the edge of the bed, finding some relief. He reports bulky, foul-smelling, greasy stools that float (steatorrhoea) and has lost 10 kg over 4 months. His blood glucose is 12 mmol/L. Abdominal exam shows mild epigastric tenderness but no rebound. He looks malnourished, with temporal wasting and dry skin.
- **Dissect the vignette:**
  - *Epigastric pain radiating to back* → classic pancreatic pain; pancreatic inflammation irritates retroperitoneal structures.
  - *Leaning forward relieves pain* → typical chronic pancreatitis position; reduces tension on the inflamed pancreas.
  - *Bulky, greasy stools* → exocrine insufficiency → lipase deficiency → fat malabsorption (steatorrhoea).
  - *Weight loss + temporal wasting* → malabsorption + catabolic state; sometimes from diabetes (type 3c).
  - *Diabetes* → progressive destruction of islet cells; insulin and glucagon both lost, making glucose control brittle.
  - *Heavy alcohol history* → most common cause in adults (60–70% of cases).
- **Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
  - **Painless chronic pancreatitis:** Up to 20% present with only steatorrhoea and diabetes, no pain (especially in autoimmune or hereditary forms). Can be misdiagnosed as new-onset diabetes or malabsorption from coeliac disease.
  - **Recurrent acute-on-chronic:** Instead of chronic dull pain, they have intermittent severe attacks mimicking acute pancreatitis; may have normal lipase between episodes because gland is scarred.
  - **Jaundice as first sign:** A benign stricture of the intrapancreatic common bile duct can cause painless obstructive jaundice, raising suspicion of pancreatic cancer. CT shows calcifications and ductal dilatation without mass.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
- **Plain-language definition:** Chronic pancreatitis is a long-standing, ongoing inflammation of the pancreas that slowly destroys the tissue. Instead of healing, the gland becomes scarred, loses its enzyme-producing cells (exocrine failure → maldigestion) and hormone-producing cells (endocrine failure → diabetes). The hallmark is irreversible structural damage (calcifications, dilated ducts).
- **Why it matters:**
  - *Frequency:* 5–12 per 100,000; prevalence rising due to alcohol and increasing detection. A common cause of hospitalisation for chronic abdominal pain.
  - *Stakes:* Life-long impairment; risk of pancreatic cancer (3–5% lifetime risk); complex, brittle diabetes; severe malnutrition; pseudocysts and bile duct obstruction.
  - *Pitfalls:* Often missed in early stages; pain can be attributed to other abdominal conditions; steatorrhoea mistaken for IBS; diabetes considered type 2. A high index of suspicion is needed in patients with risk factors and atypical GI symptoms.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
The story begins with recurrent episodes of pancreatic injury, most often from alcohol or other triggers. These cause premature activation of trypsin within the gland, leading to autodigestion, necrosis, and inflammation. With each attack, healing occurs via fibrosis, not regeneration. Repeated cycles lead to:

- **Calcifications:** Deposits of calcium carbonate form in the ducts and parenchyma, visible on CT (pathognomonic). Mechanism: destruction of epithelial cells → loss of bicarbonate and citrate inhibitors → calcium precipitation.
- **Ductal strictures and stones:** Fibrosis narrows the main pancreatic duct (MPD) and side branches; stones get trapped upstream → further ductal hypertension → pain.
- **Exocrine failure:** ≥90% of acinar tissue must be lost before steatorrhoea appears. Fat malabsorption → foul, greasy stools → weight loss. Concurrent fat-soluble vitamin deficiencies (A, D, E, K) contribute to osteoporosis, coagulopathy.
- **Endocrine failure:** Islets of Langerhans are destroyed. Loss of both β (insulin) and α (glucagon) cells → “brittle” diabetes (type 3c) with labile glucose and propensity to severe hypoglycaemia (because glucagon counterregulatory response is lost).
- **Pain mechanisms:**
  1. Ductal hypertension (obstruction and increased pressure)
  2. Parenchymal inflammation (perineural inflammation)
  3. Fibrosis stretches the capsule
  This explains why leaning forward (reducing retroperitoneal tension) or waiting for an attack to subside may partially relieve pain.

**Connecting to pattern recognition cues:**
- *Epigastric back pain* → from inflammation and ductal hypertension activating splanchnic nerves.
- *Steatorrhoea* → exocrine failure (lipase <10%).
- *Diabetes* → islet destruction.
- *CT calcifications* → direct evidence of chronic injury.
- *Weight loss* → from malabsorption, poor intake due to pain, and catabolism.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins):
| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Toxic-metabolic** | Alcohol | + Male, 40–60 y, heavy daily use (≥80g ethanol/day for 6–12 y) |
| | Smoking | Independent risk factor; accelerates progression |
| | Hypertriglyceridemia | TG >1000 mg/dL (11.3 mmol/L) — often triggers acute exacerbations |
| | Hypercalcemia | Parathyroid adenoma, multiple myeloma, vitamin D toxicity |
| | Chronic renal failure | Uremic toxins |
| **Idiopathic** | Early-onset (≤35 y) | No clear cause; often pain-predominant |
| | Late-onset (≥60 y) | More calcific; less pain |
| **Genetic** | PRSS1 (trypsinogen) mutation | Autosomal dominant; early-onset recurrent acute → chronic (especially in children/young adults) |
| | SPINK1, CFTR, CTRC mutations | Cystic fibrosis phenotype or milder variants |
| | α1-antitrypsin deficiency | Rare |
| **Autoimmune** | Type 1 (LG4-related) | + IgG4 elevation, other organs (salivary glands, biliary tree) |
| | Type 2 | + IBD (Crohn’s, UC), no IgG4; responds to steroids |
| **Recurrent severe acute pancreatitis** | Repeated attacks of AP due to gallstones, ERCP, trauma | Each attack adds fibrosis |
| **Obstructive** | Pancreas divisum | Dominant dorsal duct drains via minor papilla; relative obstruction |
| | Tumour / pseudocyst compressing duct | Pain, upstream duct dilatation |
| | Sphincter of Oddi dysfunction | Post-cholecystectomy pain |

**Most common cause:** **Alcohol** (60–70% in adults). In developing countries, tropical pancreatitis (a form of idiopathic/genetic) is more common.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria:** The diagnosis of chronic pancreatitis is usually confirmed by **imaging** plus functional evidence. Two commonly used systems: **Mayo Clinic criteria** (clinical + imaging) or **M-ANNHEIM** (includes aetiology, stage, severity). However, for everyday practice: **Any of the following is diagnostic:**
- Pancreatic calcifications on CT
- Moderate to marked ductal changes on MRCP/ERCP (Cambridge classification if needed)
- Steatorrhoea with abnormal pancreatic function test (e.g., faecal elastase <100 μg/g)

| Test | What It Shows | Why Order | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|-----------|----------------|
| **Serum lipase/amylase** | Normal or mildly elevated | Rule out acute attack; can be normal in burnt-out disease | If normal, doesn’t exclude CP; if high, suggests acute-on-chronic |
| **Abdominal CT (non-contrast + contrast)** | Calcifications (dots, linear, within ducts), dilated MPD (>3mm), atrophy, pseudocyst | Gold standard for structural diagnosis | Single finding: **Any pancreatic calcification** is highly specific for CP (except in advanced age or hypercalcemia). |
| **MRCP (magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography)** | Ductal strictures, stones, irregularities | Better for intraductal detail without radiation; can see ductal filling defects | Slight stenosis with upstream dilatation (“chain of lakes”) − classic |
| **Faecal elastase-1** | <200 μg/g = moderate exocrine insufficiency; <100 μg/g = severe | Noninvasive, does not require steatorrhoea | **Single low value confirms exocrine failure** (but can be false-positive in watery diarrhoea). |
| **3-day faecal fat** (direct) | Fat >7g/24h | Formal gold standard for steatorrhoea; rarely done | >7g = malabsorption |
| **HbA1c, fasting glucose, OGTT** | Impaired fasting/diabetes | Screen for endocrine failure | Type 3c diabetes: low C-peptide, no islet autoantibodies. HbA1c may be unreliable due to often <6.5% because of rapid turnover. |
| **IgG4** | Elevated in Type 1 AIP | Suspect autoimmune when other features atypical (no calcifications, young, jaundice) | >2x upper limit normal suggests AIP; but can be elevated in other cancers (e.g., cholangiocarcinoma). |
| **Endoscopic ultrasound (EUS)** | Hyperechogenic foci (stones), lobular parenchyma, dilated side branches | Most sensitive for early CP before calcifications appear | EUS criteria: ≥4 of 9 features for definite CP. |

**Pattern recognition clue:** In a patient with chronic abdominal pain and weight loss, **a single abdominal X-ray showing pancreatic calcifications** is virtually diagnostic of chronic pancreatitis (but CT is more sensitive).
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour – usually in A&E or ward for acute-on-chronic exacerbation):**
- **IV fluids:** Balanced crystalloid (e.g., Ringer’s lactate) at 10–15 mL/kg bolus if hypovolaemic, then 1.5 mL/kg/h. *Why:* to correct dehydration from vomiting or decreased oral intake; prevents acute kidney injury.
- **Analgesia:** IV morphine 2.5–5 mg PRN; or fentanyl 25–50 μg IV. Avoid meperidine (pethidine) – short half-life, neurotoxic metabolite. *Why:* severe pain requires potent opioids.
- **Nausea/vomiting:** IV metoclopramide 10 mg or ondansetron 4 mg.
- **Check glucose:** If >12 mmol/L, start sliding-scale insulin (dose per local protocol). *Why:* hyperglycemia worsens inflammation and dehydration.

**Ward (24–72 hours):**
- **Pain management:** Regular oral opioids (e.g., morphine SR 5–10 mg q4h) + PCA if needed. Add **gabapentin** 300–900 mg/day (start low, titrate) for neuropathic component. *Why:* chronic pain often has central sensitisation; opioids alone can cause hyperalgesia.
- **Nutrition:**
  - If severe, NPO initially, then progress to low-fat oral diet (≤30 g fat/day) + pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (**PERT**).
  - PERT: **Pancrelipase (Creon)** 25,000–50,000 units lipase per meal, 10,000–25,000 per snack. Take with *first bite* of food. *Why:* replaces missing lipase, trypsin, amylase; reduces steatorrhoea, improves weight.
  - If unable to eat (severe pain, vomiting): nasojejunal (NJ) tube feeding with semi-elemental low-fat formula, or total parenteral nutrition (TPN) if gut fails. *Why:* avoids further pancreatic stimulation (oral feeding releases CCK, worsening inflammation).
- **Glucose control:** Type 3c diabetes requires careful balance – avoid long-acting insulin; use basal-bolus with rapid analogue (e.g., insulin glargine 8-10 U once daily + insulin aspart with meals). Monitor for hypoglycaemia. *Why:* lack of glucagon makes patients prone to severe lows.
- **Alcohol cessation counselling + referral to addiction services.** *Why:* only intervention that slows progression and improves pain control.

**Long-term:**
- **Pain control:** Continue PERT; consider coeliac plexus block (via EUS) if refractory pain. *Pearl:* if pain improves with PERT alone, it suggests ductal hypertension from obstruction. If not, consider neuropathic mechanisms.
- **Exocrine supplementation:** PERT lifelong – adjust dose based on stool consistency, weight, and symptoms. Add fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) if deficiencies present.
- **Diabetes management:** Refer to endocrinology; educate about hypoglycaemia awareness; avoid sulfonylureas (risk of severe hypoglycemia). Metformin may be used if mild.
- **Surveillance for pancreatic cancer:** No formal screening universally, but consider annual CT/MRI in patients ≥50 y with family history or hereditary pancreatitis. *Reason:* risk 3–5% over 20 years (50-fold increased in hereditary forms).
- **Lifestyle:** Smoking cessation (even more important than alcohol for progression); low-fat diet; small frequent meals.
- **Surgery:** Only for intractable pain or complications (pseudocyst, biliary obstruction, disconnected duct syndrome). Options include **pancreaticojejunostomy** (Puestow procedure) for dilated duct, or **distal pancreatectomy** for disease confined to tail.

**Common pitfalls to avoid:**
- **Don’t prescribe fibrates for hypertriglyceridaemia** on the ward; use fenofibrate 160 mg/d in stable phase, but acute exacerbations require insulin infusion to lower triglycerides quickly.
- **Don’t use long-acting opioids** as sole therapy – they’re ineffective for neuropathic pain and cause tolerance.
- **Don’t diagnose diabetes as type 2** without checking C-peptide and antibodies – type 3c responds very differently.
- **Don’t stop PERT** if patient misses a meal – no need; just skip that dose.
- **Don’t give enteric-coated pancrelipase** – they must be non-enteric (like Creon) to work in the duodenum.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Chronic Pancreatitis | Pancreatic Adenocarcinoma | Acute Pancreatitis | Peptic Ulcer Disease (posterior penetrating) |
|---------|----------------------|---------------------------|-------------------|----------------------------------------------|
| **Pain** | Epigastric → back, chronic dull, relieved by leaning forward | Dull epigastric, constant, may radiate to back; often painless at first | Acute, severe epigastric, constant, radiates to back; no relief with position | Epigastric burning/gnawing, worse with meals or at night, may radiate to back if posterior penetration |
| **Exam** | Epigastric tenderness, may feel abdominal mass (pseudocyst) | Epigastric mass (±), jaundice (obstructive), supraclavicular node (Virchow’s) | Diffuse abdominal tenderness, guarding, rebound, absent bowel sounds | Epigastric tenderness only; signs of acute abdomen if perforation |
| **Imaging** | Pancreatic calcifications, ductal dilatation (“chain of lakes”), atrophy | Solid hypodense mass (head > body/tail), with vascular encasement, distal duct dilatation (double duct sign: bile duct + MPD) | Oedematous pancreas, peripancreatic fat stranding, necrosis (non-enhancing areas) | Barium meal or endoscopy: ulcer crater; CT negative for pancreatic changes |
| **Labs** | Lipase normal (or mild elevation), faecal elastase ↓, HbA1c ↑ | CA 19-9 ↑ (≤1000 U/mL), LFTs: obstructive pattern (ALP, GGT, bilirubin ↑) | Lipase/Amylase >3× upper limit | No specific labs; H. pylori positive in 90% of duodenal ulcers; occult blood positive |
| **Key differentiating factor** | **Calcifications on CT** are pathognomonic; long history of alcohol/smoking | **CA 19-9 > 1000 U/mL** or mass with metastasis; double duct sign; weight loss out of proportion to pain | **Lipase >3× ULN** and typical pain onset <6 hours; no calcifications at first attack | **Endoscopy shows ulcer**; pain pattern (meal-related); H. pylori testing |

*Other mimics:*
- **Gastrinoma (Zollinger-Ellison syndrome):** Severe peptic ulcers, diarrhoea, ↑ gastrin >1000 pg/mL; consider if CT shows only prominent gastric folds, not pancreatic pathology.
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
| Complication | How It Presents | Key Signs | What to Do |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Pancreatic pseudocyst** | Persistent epigastric pain, early satiety, palpable mass | CT shows fluid collection surrounded by fibrous wall; no enhancing lining (unlike abscess) | EUS-guided cystgastrostomy (drainage); if infected → antibiotics + drainage. *Don’t* drain percutaneously unless infected – high recurrence. |
| **Pancreatic ascites** | Distended abdomen, pain, shifting dullness | High-amylase ascitic fluid (>1000 U/L), protein >2.5 g/dL | Therapeutic paracentesis; ERCP to locate duct leak + stent placement; octreotide 100–200 μg SC q8h to reduce secretion. |
| **Bile duct stricture/obstruction** | Jaundice, pruritus, cholangitis (fever, RUQ pain) | LFTs: obstructive (ALP↑, GGT↑, bilirubin↑); MRCP shows distal CBD stricture | ERCP with stent placement; if stricture remains after 6 weeks, consider covered self-expanding metal stent (to exclude cancer). |
| **Splenic vein thrombosis** | Left upper quadrant pain, splenomegaly, variceal bleeding (gastric, not oesophageal) | Doppler ultrasound shows absent flow in splenic vein | Splenectomy if bleeding; otherwise, anticoagulation controversial – only if extension to portal vein or recurrent events. |
| **Pancreatic cancer (3–5% lifetime risk)** | Weight loss, new-onset diabetes after age 50, painless jaundice, abdominal mass | CA 19-9 ↑, CT shows mass; biopsy-confirmed | Surgical resection (Whipple) if localised; palliative chemo if metastatic. **Every new case of chronic pancreatitis should have a baseline CT to exclude cancer.** |
| **Type 3c diabetes (brittle diabetes)** | Hypoglycaemic episodes, labile glucose, no ketosis (even with hyperglycaemia) | Low C-peptide, negative autoantibodies, low glucagon response to hypoglycaemia | Insulin sliding scale; avoid secretagogues. Endocrine referral. |

**Late-presentation patterns:** A patient with untreated chronic pancreatitis may present with **pancreatic cancer** or **pseudocyst rupture** (acute abdominal pain, shock). **Missed pseudocyst** can become infected → sepsis; or erode into adjacent vessels → massive GI bleeding.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

1. **TIGAR-O** – Causes of Chronic Pancreatitis (as per section 4):
   - **T** – Toxic-metabolic (alcohol, smoking, hyper-TG, hyper-calcemia, chronic renal failure)
   - **I** – Idiopathic (early/late onset)
   - **G** – Genetic (PRSS1, SPINK1, CFTR)
   - **A** – Autoimmune (Type 1 IgG4, Type 2 IBD)
   - **R** – Recurrent severe acute pancreatitis
   - **O** – Obstructive (divisum, tumour, sphincter dysfunction)

2. **P-A-I-N** – Chronic Pancreatitis Pain Management:
   - **P** – Pancreatic enzymes (PERT)
   - **A** – Analgesics (opioids + adjuvant like gabapentin)
   - **I** – Interventions (coeliac plexus block, nerve blocks, surgeries)
   - **N** – Non-pharmacologic (lifestyle: alcohol cessation, smoking cessation, low-fat small meals)

3. **C-A-L-C** – CT hallmarks of Chronic Pancreatitis:
   - **C** – Calcifications
   - **A** – Atrophy
   - **L** – Lobar (side branch) dilatation
   - **C** – Cysts (pseudocysts)

**Clinical Pearls:**

1. **The “chain of lakes” appearance** on MRCP: multiple dilated side branches alternating with strictures in the main duct – virtually diagnostic of chronic pancreatitis.
2. **Faecal elastase <100 μg/g** is specific for severe exocrine failure; if patient is on PERT, elastase will still be low (because the measured elastase is human, not from the supplement). So it can be used even while on therapy.
3. **Diabetes in chronic pancreatitis is often misclassified:** Check C-peptide (<0.2 nmol/L suggests type 3c) and islet autoantibodies (negative). HbA1c may be deceptively low due to red cell turnover from chronic illness.
4. **Pain improving with PERT alone** suggests a ductal hypertension component – the enzyme reduces cholecystokinin (CCK) secretion via negative feedback, decreasing pancreatic secretion and ductal pressure.
5. **In autoimmune pancreatitis (Type 1), the pancreas looks “sausage-shaped”** on CT with a peripheral hypodense capsule (“rim sign”), absence of calcifications, and often associated with IgG4 elevation. It mimics pancreatic cancer but responds dramatically to steroids.
6. **Smoking accelerates fibrosis more than alcohol** – quitting smoking halves the risk of progression. This is not just about non-smoking; active smoking is the single strongest modifiable factor for pain and calcifications.
7. **Steatorrhoea requires ≥90% acinar destruction** – so if a patient has steatorrhoea, their CP is advanced. Conversely, absence of steatorrhoea does not rule out CP.

**Common exam/ward mistakes to avoid:**
- Ordering **amylase** instead of lipase (amylase is less specific and can be normal in chronic pancreatitis). Stick to lipase.
- Diagnosing acute pancreatitis **without checking lipase** in a CP patient who has acute pain; they might have acute-on-chronic but lipase can be normal if gland burnt out. Use CT instead.
- Starting a **low-fat diet** without PERT: malnutrition will worsen.
- Prescribing **glucagon for hypoglycaemia** – it may not work because α cells are destroyed. Use IV dextrose instead.
- **Not offering pain management early** – chronic pain leads to opioid dependence and depression; involve pain team early.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call your senior (registrar/consultant) or escalate to ICU:**

| Criterion | Threshold | Action |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| **NEWS2 score** | ≥ 7 (or any single parameter: RR >24, SpO2 <91% on air, systolic BP <90 mmHg, heart rate >130/min) | Immediate senior review; consider HDU/ICU |
| **Lactate** | > 3 mmol/L (especially with acidosis) | Sign of hypoperfusion/sepsis; find source – ischaemia? Infection? |
| **Organ failure** (Modified Marshall score) | CVS: SBP <90 mmHg despite fluids; **Resp: PaO2/FiO2 <300**; Renal: creatinine >170 μmol/L | ICU referral for vasopressors, ventilatory support, RRT |
| **GCS** | < 15 (especially decreasing) | Assess for hypoglycaemia, hypercapnia, sepsis, or PCA opioid toxicity |
| **Sepsis** | qSOFA ≥2 (RR ≥22, altered mentation, SBP ≤100 mmHg) | Start IV antibiotics (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam) and source control – look for infected pseudocyst or cholangitis |
| **Acute GI bleed** | Haematemesis, melaena, or severe anaemia – suspect from pseudocyst erosion into splenic artery or gastric varices (splenic vein thrombosis) | Crossmatch blood, start PPI (if secondary gastritis), arrange urgent CT angiography +/- endoscopy; call IR for possible embolisation |
| **New onset jaundice** | Bilirubin >50 μmol/L with ALP >2× ULN | Urgent MRCP/ERCP; rule out CBD stricture, stenting needed. If also CA 19-9 rising, suspect cancer. |
| **Uncontrolled pain** | Requiring >10 mg IV morphine per hour, or not controlled with PCA | Consider coeliac plexus block, palliative care consult, or surgical opinion |
| **Severe hypoglycaemia** | Glucose <2.2 mmol/L or seizure | IV 50% dextrose 25 g; if persistent, start 10% dextrose infusion; check if insulin dose too high or meal missed |

**When to call the crash team:**
- Cardiac/respiratory arrest
- Sudden profound hypotension (SBP <60 mmHg) with no response to fluids
- GCS <8
- Seizure

**Document all escaltions clearly** – time of call, response, and plan. Chronic pancreatitis patients can deteriorate rapidly from sepsis or bleeding, so have a low threshold.

rawMarkdown:
## Chronic Pancreatitis

**One-liner:** Progressive, irreversible inflammatory damage to the pancreas causing exocrine/endocrine failure; affects 5–12/100,000; pain, malabsorption, diabetes drive morbidity.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You’ll See

- **Classic vignette:** A 55-year-old man with a 20-year history of heavy alcohol use presents with severe epigastric pain radiating straight through to his back. He leans forward over the edge of the bed, finding some relief. He reports bulky, foul-smelling, greasy stools that float (steatorrhoea) and has lost 10 kg over 4 months. His blood glucose is 12 mmol/L. Abdominal exam shows mild epigastric tenderness but no rebound. He looks malnourished, with temporal wasting and dry skin.
- **Dissect the vignette:**
  - *Epigastric pain radiating to back* → classic pancreatic pain; pancreatic inflammation irritates retroperitoneal structures.
  - *Leaning forward relieves pain* → typical chronic pancreatitis position; reduces tension on the inflamed pancreas.
  - *Bulky, greasy stools* → exocrine insufficiency → lipase deficiency → fat malabsorption (steatorrhoea).
  - *Weight loss + temporal wasting* → malabsorption + catabolic state; sometimes from diabetes (type 3c).
  - *Diabetes* → progressive destruction of islet cells; insulin and glucagon both lost, making glucose control brittle.
  - *Heavy alcohol history* → most common cause in adults (60–70% of cases).
- **Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
  - **Painless chronic pancreatitis:** Up to 20% present with only steatorrhoea and diabetes, no pain (especially in autoimmune or hereditary forms). Can be misdiagnosed as new-onset diabetes or malabsorption from coeliac disease.
  - **Recurrent acute-on-chronic:** Instead of chronic dull pain, they have intermittent severe attacks mimicking acute pancreatitis; may have normal lipase between episodes because gland is scarred.
  - **Jaundice as first sign:** A benign stricture of the intrapancreatic common bile duct can cause painless obstructive jaundice, raising suspicion of pancreatic cancer. CT shows calcifications and ductal dilatation without mass.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

- **Plain-language definition:** Chronic pancreatitis is a long-standing, ongoing inflammation of the pancreas that slowly destroys the tissue. Instead of healing, the gland becomes scarred, loses its enzyme-producing cells (exocrine failure → maldigestion) and hormone-producing cells (endocrine failure → diabetes). The hallmark is irreversible structural damage (calcifications, dilated ducts).
- **Why it matters:**
  - *Frequency:* 5–12 per 100,000; prevalence rising due to alcohol and increasing detection. A common cause of hospitalisation for chronic abdominal pain.
  - *Stakes:* Life-long impairment; risk of pancreatic cancer (3–5% lifetime risk); complex, brittle diabetes; severe malnutrition; pseudocysts and bile duct obstruction.
  - *Pitfalls:* Often missed in early stages; pain can be attributed to other abdominal conditions; steatorrhoea mistaken for IBS; diabetes considered type 2. A high index of suspicion is needed in patients with risk factors and atypical GI symptoms.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

The story begins with recurrent episodes of pancreatic injury, most often from alcohol or other triggers. These cause premature activation of trypsin within the gland, leading to autodigestion, necrosis, and inflammation. With each attack, healing occurs via fibrosis, not regeneration. Repeated cycles lead to:

- **Calcifications:** Deposits of calcium carbonate form in the ducts and parenchyma, visible on CT (pathognomonic). Mechanism: destruction of epithelial cells → loss of bicarbonate and citrate inhibitors → calcium precipitation.
- **Ductal strictures and stones:** Fibrosis narrows the main pancreatic duct (MPD) and side branches; stones get trapped upstream → further ductal hypertension → pain.
- **Exocrine failure:** ≥90% of acinar tissue must be lost before steatorrhoea appears. Fat malabsorption → foul, greasy stools → weight loss. Concurrent fat-soluble vitamin deficiencies (A, D, E, K) contribute to osteoporosis, coagulopathy.
- **Endocrine failure:** Islets of Langerhans are destroyed. Loss of both β (insulin) and α (glucagon) cells → “brittle” diabetes (type 3c) with labile glucose and propensity to severe hypoglycaemia (because glucagon counterregulatory response is lost).
- **Pain mechanisms:**
  1. Ductal hypertension (obstruction and increased pressure)
  2. Parenchymal inflammation (perineural inflammation)
  3. Fibrosis stretches the capsule
  This explains why leaning forward (reducing retroperitoneal tension) or waiting for an attack to subside may partially relieve pain.

**Connecting to pattern recognition cues:**
- *Epigastric back pain* → from inflammation and ductal hypertension activating splanchnic nerves.
- *Steatorrhoea* → exocrine failure (lipase <10%).
- *Diabetes* → islet destruction.
- *CT calcifications* → direct evidence of chronic injury.
- *Weight loss* → from malabsorption, poor intake due to pain, and catabolism.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins)

| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Toxic-metabolic** | Alcohol | + Male, 40–60 y, heavy daily use (≥80g ethanol/day for 6–12 y) |
| | Smoking | Independent risk factor; accelerates progression |
| | Hypertriglyceridemia | TG >1000 mg/dL (11.3 mmol/L) — often triggers acute exacerbations |
| | Hypercalcemia | Parathyroid adenoma, multiple myeloma, vitamin D toxicity |
| | Chronic renal failure | Uremic toxins |
| **Idiopathic** | Early-onset (≤35 y) | No clear cause; often pain-predominant |
| | Late-onset (≥60 y) | More calcific; less pain |
| **Genetic** | PRSS1 (trypsinogen) mutation | Autosomal dominant; early-onset recurrent acute → chronic (especially in children/young adults) |
| | SPINK1, CFTR, CTRC mutations | Cystic fibrosis phenotype or milder variants |
| | α1-antitrypsin deficiency | Rare |
| **Autoimmune** | Type 1 (LG4-related) | + IgG4 elevation, other organs (salivary glands, biliary tree) |
| | Type 2 | + IBD (Crohn’s, UC), no IgG4; responds to steroids |
| **Recurrent severe acute pancreatitis** | Repeated attacks of AP due to gallstones, ERCP, trauma | Each attack adds fibrosis |
| **Obstructive** | Pancreas divisum | Dominant dorsal duct drains via minor papilla; relative obstruction |
| | Tumour / pseudocyst compressing duct | Pain, upstream duct dilatation |
| | Sphincter of Oddi dysfunction | Post-cholecystectomy pain |

**Most common cause:** **Alcohol** (60–70% in adults). In developing countries, tropical pancreatitis (a form of idiopathic/genetic) is more common.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria:** The diagnosis of chronic pancreatitis is usually confirmed by **imaging** plus functional evidence. Two commonly used systems: **Mayo Clinic criteria** (clinical + imaging) or **M-ANNHEIM** (includes aetiology, stage, severity). However, for everyday practice: **Any of the following is diagnostic:**
- Pancreatic calcifications on CT
- Moderate to marked ductal changes on MRCP/ERCP (Cambridge classification if needed)
- Steatorrhoea with abnormal pancreatic function test (e.g., faecal elastase <100 μg/g)

| Test | What It Shows | Why Order | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|-----------|----------------|
| **Serum lipase/amylase** | Normal or mildly elevated | Rule out acute attack; can be normal in burnt-out disease | If normal, doesn’t exclude CP; if high, suggests acute-on-chronic |
| **Abdominal CT (non-contrast + contrast)** | Calcifications (dots, linear, within ducts), dilated MPD (>3mm), atrophy, pseudocyst | Gold standard for structural diagnosis | Single finding: **Any pancreatic calcification** is highly specific for CP (except in advanced age or hypercalcemia). |
| **MRCP (magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography)** | Ductal strictures, stones, irregularities | Better for intraductal detail without radiation; can see ductal filling defects | Slight stenosis with upstream dilatation (“chain of lakes”) − classic |
| **Faecal elastase-1** | <200 μg/g = moderate exocrine insufficiency; <100 μg/g = severe | Noninvasive, does not require steatorrhoea | **Single low value confirms exocrine failure** (but can be false-positive in watery diarrhoea). |
| **3-day faecal fat** (direct) | Fat >7g/24h | Formal gold standard for steatorrhoea; rarely done | >7g = malabsorption |
| **HbA1c, fasting glucose, OGTT** | Impaired fasting/diabetes | Screen for endocrine failure | Type 3c diabetes: low C-peptide, no islet autoantibodies. HbA1c may be unreliable due to often <6.5% because of rapid turnover. |
| **IgG4** | Elevated in Type 1 AIP | Suspect autoimmune when other features atypical (no calcifications, young, jaundice) | >2x upper limit normal suggests AIP; but can be elevated in other cancers (e.g., cholangiocarcinoma). |
| **Endoscopic ultrasound (EUS)** | Hyperechogenic foci (stones), lobular parenchyma, dilated side branches | Most sensitive for early CP before calcifications appear | EUS criteria: ≥4 of 9 features for definite CP. |

**Pattern recognition clue:** In a patient with chronic abdominal pain and weight loss, **a single abdominal X-ray showing pancreatic calcifications** is virtually diagnostic of chronic pancreatitis (but CT is more sensitive).

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour – usually in A&E or ward for acute-on-chronic exacerbation):**
- **IV fluids:** Balanced crystalloid (e.g., Ringer’s lactate) at 10–15 mL/kg bolus if hypovolaemic, then 1.5 mL/kg/h. *Why:* to correct dehydration from vomiting or decreased oral intake; prevents acute kidney injury.
- **Analgesia:** IV morphine 2.5–5 mg PRN; or fentanyl 25–50 μg IV. Avoid meperidine (pethidine) – short half-life, neurotoxic metabolite. *Why:* severe pain requires potent opioids.
- **Nausea/vomiting:** IV metoclopramide 10 mg or ondansetron 4 mg.
- **Check glucose:** If >12 mmol/L, start sliding-scale insulin (dose per local protocol). *Why:* hyperglycemia worsens inflammation and dehydration.

**Ward (24–72 hours):**
- **Pain management:** Regular oral opioids (e.g., morphine SR 5–10 mg q4h) + PCA if needed. Add **gabapentin** 300–900 mg/day (start low, titrate) for neuropathic component. *Why:* chronic pain often has central sensitisation; opioids alone can cause hyperalgesia.
- **Nutrition:**
  - If severe, NPO initially, then progress to low-fat oral diet (≤30 g fat/day) + pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (**PERT**).
  - PERT: **Pancrelipase (Creon)** 25,000–50,000 units lipase per meal, 10,000–25,000 per snack. Take with *first bite* of food. *Why:* replaces missing lipase, trypsin, amylase; reduces steatorrhoea, improves weight.
  - If unable to eat (severe pain, vomiting): nasojejunal (NJ) tube feeding with semi-elemental low-fat formula, or total parenteral nutrition (TPN) if gut fails. *Why:* avoids further pancreatic stimulation (oral feeding releases CCK, worsening inflammation).
- **Glucose control:** Type 3c diabetes requires careful balance – avoid long-acting insulin; use basal-bolus with rapid analogue (e.g., insulin glargine 8-10 U once daily + insulin aspart with meals). Monitor for hypoglycaemia. *Why:* lack of glucagon makes patients prone to severe lows.
- **Alcohol cessation counselling + referral to addiction services.** *Why:* only intervention that slows progression and improves pain control.

**Long-term:**
- **Pain control:** Continue PERT; consider coeliac plexus block (via EUS) if refractory pain. *Pearl:* if pain improves with PERT alone, it suggests ductal hypertension from obstruction. If not, consider neuropathic mechanisms.
- **Exocrine supplementation:** PERT lifelong – adjust dose based on stool consistency, weight, and symptoms. Add fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) if deficiencies present.
- **Diabetes management:** Refer to endocrinology; educate about hypoglycaemia awareness; avoid sulfonylureas (risk of severe hypoglycemia). Metformin may be used if mild.
- **Surveillance for pancreatic cancer:** No formal screening universally, but consider annual CT/MRI in patients ≥50 y with family history or hereditary pancreatitis. *Reason:* risk 3–5% over 20 years (50-fold increased in hereditary forms).
- **Lifestyle:** Smoking cessation (even more important than alcohol for progression); low-fat diet; small frequent meals.
- **Surgery:** Only for intractable pain or complications (pseudocyst, biliary obstruction, disconnected duct syndrome). Options include **pancreaticojejunostomy** (Puestow procedure) for dilated duct, or **distal pancreatectomy** for disease confined to tail.

**Common pitfalls to avoid:**
- **Don’t prescribe fibrates for hypertriglyceridaemia** on the ward; use fenofibrate 160 mg/d in stable phase, but acute exacerbations require insulin infusion to lower triglycerides quickly.
- **Don’t use long-acting opioids** as sole therapy – they’re ineffective for neuropathic pain and cause tolerance.
- **Don’t diagnose diabetes as type 2** without checking C-peptide and antibodies – type 3c responds very differently.
- **Don’t stop PERT** if patient misses a meal – no need; just skip that dose.
- **Don’t give enteric-coated pancrelipase** – they must be non-enteric (like Creon) to work in the duodenum.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Chronic Pancreatitis | Pancreatic Adenocarcinoma | Acute Pancreatitis | Peptic Ulcer Disease (posterior penetrating) |
|---------|----------------------|---------------------------|-------------------|----------------------------------------------|
| **Pain** | Epigastric → back, chronic dull, relieved by leaning forward | Dull epigastric, constant, may radiate to back; often painless at first | Acute, severe epigastric, constant, radiates to back; no relief with position | Epigastric burning/gnawing, worse with meals or at night, may radiate to back if posterior penetration |
| **Exam** | Epigastric tenderness, may feel abdominal mass (pseudocyst) | Epigastric mass (±), jaundice (obstructive), supraclavicular node (Virchow’s) | Diffuse abdominal tenderness, guarding, rebound, absent bowel sounds | Epigastric tenderness only; signs of acute abdomen if perforation |
| **Imaging** | Pancreatic calcifications, ductal dilatation (“chain of lakes”), atrophy | Solid hypodense mass (head > body/tail), with vascular encasement, distal duct dilatation (double duct sign: bile duct + MPD) | Oedematous pancreas, peripancreatic fat stranding, necrosis (non-enhancing areas) | Barium meal or endoscopy: ulcer crater; CT negative for pancreatic changes |
| **Labs** | Lipase normal (or mild elevation), faecal elastase ↓, HbA1c ↑ | CA 19-9 ↑ (≤1000 U/mL), LFTs: obstructive pattern (ALP, GGT, bilirubin ↑) | Lipase/Amylase >3× upper limit | No specific labs; H. pylori positive in 90% of duodenal ulcers; occult blood positive |
| **Key differentiating factor** | **Calcifications on CT** are pathognomonic; long history of alcohol/smoking | **CA 19-9 > 1000 U/mL** or mass with metastasis; double duct sign; weight loss out of proportion to pain | **Lipase >3× ULN** and typical pain onset <6 hours; no calcifications at first attack | **Endoscopy shows ulcer**; pain pattern (meal-related); H. pylori testing |

*Other mimics:*
- **Gastrinoma (Zollinger-Ellison syndrome):** Severe peptic ulcers, diarrhoea, ↑ gastrin >1000 pg/mL; consider if CT shows only prominent gastric folds, not pancreatic pathology.

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

| Complication | How It Presents | Key Signs | What to Do |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Pancreatic pseudocyst** | Persistent epigastric pain, early satiety, palpable mass | CT shows fluid collection surrounded by fibrous wall; no enhancing lining (unlike abscess) | EUS-guided cystgastrostomy (drainage); if infected → antibiotics + drainage. *Don’t* drain percutaneously unless infected – high recurrence. |
| **Pancreatic ascites** | Distended abdomen, pain, shifting dullness | High-amylase ascitic fluid (>1000 U/L), protein >2.5 g/dL | Therapeutic paracentesis; ERCP to locate duct leak + stent placement; octreotide 100–200 μg SC q8h to reduce secretion. |
| **Bile duct stricture/obstruction** | Jaundice, pruritus, cholangitis (fever, RUQ pain) | LFTs: obstructive (ALP↑, GGT↑, bilirubin↑); MRCP shows distal CBD stricture | ERCP with stent placement; if stricture remains after 6 weeks, consider covered self-expanding metal stent (to exclude cancer). |
| **Splenic vein thrombosis** | Left upper quadrant pain, splenomegaly, variceal bleeding (gastric, not oesophageal) | Doppler ultrasound shows absent flow in splenic vein | Splenectomy if bleeding; otherwise, anticoagulation controversial – only if extension to portal vein or recurrent events. |
| **Pancreatic cancer (3–5% lifetime risk)** | Weight loss, new-onset diabetes after age 50, painless jaundice, abdominal mass | CA 19-9 ↑, CT shows mass; biopsy-confirmed | Surgical resection (Whipple) if localised; palliative chemo if metastatic. **Every new case of chronic pancreatitis should have a baseline CT to exclude cancer.** |
| **Type 3c diabetes (brittle diabetes)** | Hypoglycaemic episodes, labile glucose, no ketosis (even with hyperglycaemia) | Low C-peptide, negative autoantibodies, low glucagon response to hypoglycaemia | Insulin sliding scale; avoid secretagogues. Endocrine referral. |

**Late-presentation patterns:** A patient with untreated chronic pancreatitis may present with **pancreatic cancer** or **pseudocyst rupture** (acute abdominal pain, shock). **Missed pseudocyst** can become infected → sepsis; or erode into adjacent vessels → massive GI bleeding.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

1. **TIGAR-O** – Causes of Chronic Pancreatitis (as per section 4):
   - **T** – Toxic-metabolic (alcohol, smoking, hyper-TG, hyper-calcemia, chronic renal failure)
   - **I** – Idiopathic (early/late onset)
   - **G** – Genetic (PRSS1, SPINK1, CFTR)
   - **A** – Autoimmune (Type 1 IgG4, Type 2 IBD)
   - **R** – Recurrent severe acute pancreatitis
   - **O** – Obstructive (divisum, tumour, sphincter dysfunction)

2. **P-A-I-N** – Chronic Pancreatitis Pain Management:
   - **P** – Pancreatic enzymes (PERT)
   - **A** – Analgesics (opioids + adjuvant like gabapentin)
   - **I** – Interventions (coeliac plexus block, nerve blocks, surgeries)
   - **N** – Non-pharmacologic (lifestyle: alcohol cessation, smoking cessation, low-fat small meals)

3. **C-A-L-C** – CT hallmarks of Chronic Pancreatitis:
   - **C** – Calcifications
   - **A** – Atrophy
   - **L** – Lobar (side branch) dilatation
   - **C** – Cysts (pseudocysts)

**Clinical Pearls:**

1. **The “chain of lakes” appearance** on MRCP: multiple dilated side branches alternating with strictures in the main duct – virtually diagnostic of chronic pancreatitis.
2. **Faecal elastase <100 μg/g** is specific for severe exocrine failure; if patient is on PERT, elastase will still be low (because the measured elastase is human, not from the supplement). So it can be used even while on therapy.
3. **Diabetes in chronic pancreatitis is often misclassified:** Check C-peptide (<0.2 nmol/L suggests type 3c) and islet autoantibodies (negative). HbA1c may be deceptively low due to red cell turnover from chronic illness.
4. **Pain improving with PERT alone** suggests a ductal hypertension component – the enzyme reduces cholecystokinin (CCK) secretion via negative feedback, decreasing pancreatic secretion and ductal pressure.
5. **In autoimmune pancreatitis (Type 1), the pancreas looks “sausage-shaped”** on CT with a peripheral hypodense capsule (“rim sign”), absence of calcifications, and often associated with IgG4 elevation. It mimics pancreatic cancer but responds dramatically to steroids.
6. **Smoking accelerates fibrosis more than alcohol** – quitting smoking halves the risk of progression. This is not just about non-smoking; active smoking is the single strongest modifiable factor for pain and calcifications.
7. **Steatorrhoea requires ≥90% acinar destruction** – so if a patient has steatorrhoea, their CP is advanced. Conversely, absence of steatorrhoea does not rule out CP.

**Common exam/ward mistakes to avoid:**
- Ordering **amylase** instead of lipase (amylase is less specific and can be normal in chronic pancreatitis). Stick to lipase.
- Diagnosing acute pancreatitis **without checking lipase** in a CP patient who has acute pain; they might have acute-on-chronic but lipase can be normal if gland burnt out. Use CT instead.
- Starting a **low-fat diet** without PERT: malnutrition will worsen.
- Prescribing **glucagon for hypoglycaemia** – it may not work because α cells are destroyed. Use IV dextrose instead.
- **Not offering pain management early** – chronic pain leads to opioid dependence and depression; involve pain team early.

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call your senior (registrar/consultant) or escalate to ICU:**

| Criterion | Threshold | Action |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| **NEWS2 score** | ≥ 7 (or any single parameter: RR >24, SpO2 <91% on air, systolic BP <90 mmHg, heart rate >130/min) | Immediate senior review; consider HDU/ICU |
| **Lactate** | > 3 mmol/L (especially with acidosis) | Sign of hypoperfusion/sepsis; find source – ischaemia? Infection? |
| **Organ failure** (Modified Marshall score) | CVS: SBP <90 mmHg despite fluids; **Resp: PaO2/FiO2 <300**; Renal: creatinine >170 μmol/L | ICU referral for vasopressors, ventilatory support, RRT |
| **GCS** | < 15 (especially decreasing) | Assess for hypoglycaemia, hypercapnia, sepsis, or PCA opioid toxicity |
| **Sepsis** | qSOFA ≥2 (RR ≥22, altered mentation, SBP ≤100 mmHg) | Start IV antibiotics (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam) and source control – look for infected pseudocyst or cholangitis |
| **Acute GI bleed** | Haematemesis, melaena, or severe anaemia – suspect from pseudocyst erosion into splenic artery or gastric varices (splenic vein thrombosis) | Crossmatch blood, start PPI (if secondary gastritis), arrange urgent CT angiography +/- endoscopy; call IR for possible embolisation |
| **New onset jaundice** | Bilirubin >50 μmol/L with ALP >2× ULN | Urgent MRCP/ERCP; rule out CBD stricture, stenting needed. If also CA 19-9 rising, suspect cancer. |
| **Uncontrolled pain** | Requiring >10 mg IV morphine per hour, or not controlled with PCA | Consider coeliac plexus block, palliative care consult, or surgical opinion |
| **Severe hypoglycaemia** | Glucose <2.2 mmol/L or seizure | IV 50% dextrose 25 g; if persistent, start 10% dextrose infusion; check if insulin dose too high or meal missed |

**When to call the crash team:**
- Cardiac/respiratory arrest
- Sudden profound hypotension (SBP <60 mmHg) with no response to fluids
- GCS <8
- Seizure

**Document all escaltions clearly** – time of call, response, and plan. Chronic pancreatitis patients can deteriorate rapidly from sepsis or bleeding, so have a low threshold.
